item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an aside, I love the pun in the title - "hydrophobic dog" was once upon a time a common synonym for "rabid dog". These dogs are literally hydrophobic in the chemist's sense of the term.

This SCP becomes both horrifying and sad once you realize these are all traditionally rescue/service dogs. It's most apparent with the seeing-eye and bomb-detection dogs, but St. Bernards are used in rescuing people stranded in the mountains, and Labradors are excellent swimmers and are sometimes used as lifeguard dogs. These dogs can't do their job because of their anomaly.

What's even scarier is, as seen with the picture of the dogs and indicated with the guide dog and bomb-detection dog - these dogs (breeds that are known for their protectiveness) all had to watch their owners/handlers die whilst they couldn't do a damn thing to save them.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first two images that were used in this article were non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). The first one could not be sourced, and is assumed to be non-compliant. The second image comes from here, with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, these images have been removed. The last image was created for the article, so it's cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last addendum reminded me of a time my two dogs found a litter of kittens that a stray cat had birthed in my ceiling before I did… it wasn't pretty. Having said that, the idea of this skip may be simple, but it's done really well and it doesn't leave any loose end. Satisfying and really interesting, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well written and an intriguing idea nonetheless. I read this a while ago and wanted to comment how well I thought it was, but I didn't have an account at the time. So here you go
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ughhh I knew it would break my heart by the end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did you redact those words/numbers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, there's no reason to have constant surveillance on them, much like there's no reason to have constant surveillance on dogs at a regular kennel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a really cute and well-written SCP, but the last addendum kinda bothers me. It kind of breaks suspension of disbelief that the Foundation wouldn't have people surveying SCP objects 24/7, even Safe ones.

Novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We read Where the Red Fern Grows, and it's been many a year on To Kill a Mockingbird. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image of the puppies clawing at the pool surface while their siblings are drowning below got a pretty significant reaction out of me, +1 you soulless monster

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's kind of obvious that -4 has all of its legs in the first picture.

I'm not so sure, looking closely. It depends on if it's facing forward, or if it's looking back over its shoulder- its mottled coloration makes it hard to tell what's highlight and what's fur color so I can't decide myself. Either way it's standing tripod style, but that would give a clue as to whether it's just got one foot lifted to where we can't see it or if it's actually missing a leg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't be the only person who has read or seen Old Yeller, or To Kill a Mockingbird…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This becomes horrifying and sad once you realize these are all traditionally rescue/service dogs. It's most apparent with the seeing-eye and bomb-detection dogs, but St. Bernards are used in rescuing people stranded in the mountains, and laboradors are excellent swimmers. These dogs can't do their job because of their anomaly.

Upvoted for sadness, but one criticism I can make is that it's kind of obvious that -4 has all of its legs in the first picture.

E: one last thing: I'm curious as to what -8 is. You mention it being found with -7 and then it's not brought up again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All bodily fluids expelled by SCP-2751 instances are powdery and lack liquid.

This really doesn't sound right for a scientific description.

Regardless, +1

(Also Re: Sirpudding, I'd never heard hydrophobic dog under that usage until you mentioned it, I only knew the chemistry term. I just assumed the SCP was based on the pictures. I'll bow to the author, though.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jokes can be made. My only real criticism is that the first two pictures are super-obvious in terms of being explainable. The article is good enough to support them as actual supplemental material, though, and the final addendum really bothered me in a good way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The hydrophobic effect in that case is nanoscale.

I personally think it's not that they don't notice the literal puns so much as they try to ignore them and the existential despair they trigger. (I've actually been percolating on a tale to this effect.)

If I was a Foundation researcher, I would want to know if the pun is an indication of intent. My initial hypothesis here would be anart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and never with macroscale things like dogs

Well, never to this extreme anyway.

I personally think it's not that they don't notice the literal puns so much as they try to ignore them and the existential despair they trigger. (I've actually been percolating on a tale to this effect.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hydrophobic" in chemistry means what is happening here (and never with macroscale things like dogs).

In medicine, it is the fear of water as a symptom of rabies, and "hydrophobic dog" was once upon a time a common synonym for "rabid dog", like in Old Yeller.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a pun here? I didn't get it; I just assumed that it was based off of those pictures.

Anyways, this is good.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Someone make a plaque out of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All bodily fluids expelled by SCP-2751 instances are powdery and lack liquid.

Found with SCP-2751-13 traversing the Tyrrhenian Sea

these are some salty sea dogs, +1

"February 15 2017 will be remembered as that day where a couple cool skips came into existence and they were cool and stuff and some people liked them and some didn't but the people who didn't like them did not outnumber the people who liked them, who, on the contrary, outnumbered them." -Bluemorpho 2017

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tags removed for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you should remove the tags and wait for the tag team to tag it.

There are only two tags and while they are accurate there should be more then just two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well executed, great concept. Solid +1. Also, I changed a word I think was a typo: "although they were solids" > "as if they were solids." Great first SCP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't bad, but I have trouble with suspension of disbelief with articles that are elaborate puns but apparently nobody in-universe notices. No-vote on that basis, I think.

ETA: Well I guess I was wrong about the pun hurting this article's chances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's written well and I like little background snippets. Normally I'd dislike a simple concept like this but the execution makes up for it quite well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmph, i was about to say that last picture didn't fit, but then i got what was going on in it. :(

I think you did the concept nicely for a first SCP, and the pictures didn't look incredibly photoshopped.

I might just have a dog bias, but +1

Edit: That is also not all of the tags for this article. Please let staff take care of the tagging if you don't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After three years on the wiki, this is my first SCP. We'll see how it goes.
